name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ ٱلثَّقَلَانِ (٣١)
```

```english
Soon We shall make Ourselves unoccupied for you, O you notable two! (31)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٢)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (32)
```

```arabic
يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَن تَنفُذُوا۟ مِنْ أَقْطَارِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنفُذُوا۟ ۚ لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ (٣٣)
```

```english
O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass through. But you will not pass through except by an authority [from Allah]. (33)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٤)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (34)
```

```arabic
يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ مِّن نَّارٍ وَنُحَاسٌ فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ (٣٥)
```

```english
There will be unleashed upon you a flash of fire and a smoke; then you will not be able to help one another. (35)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٦)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (36)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا ٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَٱلدِّهَانِ (٣٧)
```

```english
When the sky is split open, and turns crimson like tanned leather. (37)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٣٨)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (38)
```

```arabic
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٣٩)
```

```english
On that day neither humans will be questioned about their sins nor jinn. (39)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٠)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (40)
```

```arabic
يُعْرَفُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِٱلنَّوَٰصِى وَٱلْأَقْدَامِ (٤١)
```

```english
The guilty will be recognized by their mark; so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet. (41)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٢)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (42)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
‘This is the hell which the guilty would deny!’ (43)
```

```arabic
يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ ءَانٍ (٤٤)
```

```english
They shall circuit between it and boiling hot water. (44)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٥)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (45)
```

```arabic
وَلِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ جَنَّتَانِ (٤٦)
```

```english
For him who stands in awe of his Lord will be two gardens. (46)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٧)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (47)
```

```arabic
ذَوَاتَآ أَفْنَانٍ (٤٨)
```

```english
Both abounding in branches. (48)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٤٩)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (49)
```

```arabic
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ تَجْرِيَانِ (٥٠)
```

```english
In both of them will be two flowing springs. (50)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥١)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (51)
```

```arabic
فِيهِمَا مِن كُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ زَوْجَانِ (٥٢)
```

```english
In both of them will be two kinds of every fruit. (52)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٣)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (53)
```

```arabic
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍۭ بَطَآئِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ (٥٤)
```

```english
[They will be] reclining on beds lined with green silk. And the fruit of the two gardens will be near at hand. (54)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٥)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (55)
```

```arabic
فِيهِنَّ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٥٦)
```

```english
In them are maidens of restrained glances, whom no human has touched before, nor jinn. (56)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٧)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (57)
```

```arabic
كَأَنَّهُنَّ ٱلْيَاقُوتُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ (٥٨)
```

```english
As though they were rubies and corals. (58)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٥٩)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (59)
```

```arabic
هَلْ جَزَآءُ ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ إِلَّا ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنُ (٦٠)
```

```english
Is the requital of goodness anything but goodness? (60)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦١)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (61)
```

```arabic
وَمِن دُونِهِمَا جَنَّتَانِ (٦٢)
```

```english
Beside these two, there will be two [other] gardens. (62)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٣)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (63)
```

```arabic
مُدْهَآمَّتَانِ (٦٤)
```

```english
Dark green. (64)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٥)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (65)
```

```arabic
فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ نَضَّاخَتَانِ (٦٦)
```

```english
In both of them will be two gushing springs. (66)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٧)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (67)
```

```arabic
فِيهِمَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ (٦٨)
```

```english
In both of them will be fruits, date-palms and pomegranates. (68)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٦٩)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (69)
```

```arabic
فِيهِنَّ خَيْرَٰتٌ حِسَانٌ (٧٠)
```

```english
In them are maidens good and lovely. (70)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧١)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (71)
```

```arabic
حُورٌ مَّقْصُورَٰتٌ فِى ٱلْخِيَامِ (٧٢)
```

```english
Houris secluded in pavilions. (72)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٣)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (73)
```

```arabic
لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (٧٤)
```

```english
Whom no human has touched before, nor jinn. (74)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٥)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (75)
```

```arabic
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِىٍّ حِسَانٍ (٧٦)
```

```english
Reclining on green cushions and lovely carpets. (76)
```

```arabic
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ (٧٧)
```

```english
So which of your Lord’s bounties will you both deny? (77)
```

```arabic
تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِى ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ (٧٨)
```

```english
Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Majestic and the Munificent! (78)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the second section of the verses of this Surah. It describes the second stage of the existence of the two heavy weights (الثقلان) — *the jinn and mankind* — which is the stage of returning to Allah and the recompense for deeds. It enumerates the favors of Allah (آلاء الله) upon them, just as the preceding verses formed the first section describing the first stage of existence and enumerating Allah’s favors therein.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ الثَّقَلانِ** — *We will soon attend to you, O you two heavy weights! (55:31)* It is said: "So-and-so became free (فرغ) for such-and-such a matter" when he was previously occupied with other affairs, then left them and restricted his engagement to that specific matter out of concern for it.

Thus, the meaning of "We will soon attend to you (سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ)" is: We shall fold up the carpet of the first life and occupy Ourselves with you. The subsequent verses clarify that this "occupation" with them refers to their resurrection, their reckoning, and the recompense for their deeds, whether good or evil. Therefore, "attending to them" (الفراغ لهم) is a metaphor (استعارة) by way of metonymy (الكناية) for the transition of the stages of existence.

This "attending to them" does not contradict the fact that no task distracts Him, the Exalted, from another task (لا يشغله شأن عن شأن). For the mentioned "attending" looks toward the transition of the stage of existence, while the fact that no task distracts Him looks toward the absolute nature and vastness of His power (القدرة). Similarly, the fact that He, the Exalted, is every day in a new state (كل يوم هو في شأن) — which looks toward the diversity of states — does not contradict the fact that no task distracts Him from another.

The "two heavy weights" (الثقلان) are the jinn and mankind. The plural pronouns in "to you" (لَكُمْ) and "if you can" (إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ) and others refer to them because they are two groups comprising many individuals.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطارِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا** — *O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass! (55:33)* etc. This address — as indicated by the context — is one of the addresses of the Day of Resurrection, and it is a challenge intended to demonstrate incapacity (خطاب تعجيزي).

What is meant by "ability" (الاستطاعة) is power (القدرة), and by "passing through" (النفوذ) the confines is fleeing. "Confines" (الأقطار) is the plural of *qutr* (قطر), which means a side or region (الناحية).

The meaning is: O company of jinn and humans — and the jinn are mentioned first because they are more capable of swift movement — if you have the power to flee by passing through the regions of the heavens and the earth, exiting the kingdom of Allah and escaping His reckoning, then flee and pass through.

His saying: **لا تَنْفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطانٍ** — *But you will not pass through except by [His] authority (55:33)* means: you are not capable of passing through except by a type of authority (سلطة) over that, and you possess none. Authority (السلطان) here refers to existential power (القدرة الوجودية); it can also mean a proof (البرهان), an absolute argument (الحجة), or sovereignty (الملك).

It has been said that the "passing through" denied in the verse refers to scientific penetration (النفوذ العلمي) into the heavens and the earth from their regions, but you have seen that the context does not support this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُما شُواظٌ مِنْ نارٍ وَنُحاسٌ فَلا تَنْتَصِرانِ** — *There will be unleashed upon you a flame of fire and a [molten] copper, and you will not be able to help each other (55:35).* The term *shuwādh* (الشواظ) — as mentioned by al-Raghib — is a flame without smoke. Close to this is what is in *al-Majma‘*: that it is the green flame that detaches from the fire. *Nuhās* (النحاس) is smoke; al-Raghib said it is a flame without smoke. The meaning is clear.

His saying: **فَلا تَنْتَصِرانِ** — *and you will not be able to help each other (55:35)* means you will not assist one another such that some of you help others to remove the affliction or escape the hardship, for the efficacy of causes (الأسباب) will have ceased, and there is no protector today from Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذَا انْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَكانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهانِ** — *When the sky is split open and turns rosy like red hide (55:37)* meaning it becomes red like *dihān* (الدهان), which is red leather (الأديم الأحمر).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُسْئَلُ عَنْ ذَنْبِهِ إِنْسٌ وَلا جَانٌ** — *On that day, neither human nor jinn will be questioned about his sin (55:39).* This verse and those following it until the end of the Surah describe the reckoning and the recompense; they describe the state of the guilty (المجرمين) and those who fear the station of their Lord, and what they shall ultimately reach.

Furthermore, the verse describes the speed of the reckoning, and He, the Exalted, has said: **وَاللهُ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ** — *and Allah is swift at reckoning (24:39).*

What is meant by "that day" is the Day of Resurrection. The questioning that is denied is the familiar type of questioning. The denial of questioning in this verse does not contradict its affirmation in His saying: **وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ** — *[And say,] ‘Stop them, for they are to be questioned’ (37:24)* and His saying: **فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — *By your Lord, We will question them all (16:92).* This is because that Day has different stations (مواقف): in some they are questioned, in some their mouths are sealed and their limbs speak, and in some they are recognized by their marks (السيماء).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُعْرَفُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيماهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ** — *The guilty will be recognized by their marks; so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet (55:41).* This is in the position of answering an implied question, as if it were asked: "If they are not questioned about their sins, what will be done with them?" The answer is that the guilty will be recognized by their marks, etc. For this reason, the sentence is independent and not joined by a conjunction. What is meant by their "marks" (سيماهم) is the prominent signs on their faces.

His saying: **فَيُؤْخَذُ بِالنَّواصِي وَالْأَقْدامِ** — *so they will be seized by their forelocks and feet (55:41)* is a statement branching from the aforementioned recognition. "Forelocks" (النواصي) is the plural of *nāsiyah* (ناصية), which is the hair on the front of the head. "Feet" (الأقدام) is the plural of *qadam* (قدم). The phrase "by the forelocks" (بِالنَّواصِي) acts as the pro-agent (نائب فاعل) of "seized" (يؤخذ).

The meaning is: No one will be questioned about his sin; rather, the guilty will be recognized by the signs apparent on their faces, and the guilty will be seized by their forelocks and feet and cast into the Fire.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هذِهِ جَهَنَّمُ الَّتِي يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — *This is the hell that the guilty used to deny (55:43)* until His saying: **آنٍ** — *boiling (55:44).* This is the content of an implied speech, meaning: it will be said on that day, "This is the hell that the guilty used to deny." Al-Tabarsi said: "It is possible that when Allah, the Glorified, informed that they would be seized by the forelocks and feet, He said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'This is the hell that the guilty among your people used to deny; they shall soon enter it, so let their affair not weigh heavily upon you.'" End quote.

*Hamīm* (الحميم) is hot water, and *ān* (الآني) is that which has reached its ultimate heat. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ** — *For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46).* This is the beginning of the description of the state of the felicitous among those who fear the station of their Lord. "Station" (المقام) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "standing" (القيام), annexed to its agent. What is meant is His, the Exalted, standing over him regarding his deeds—which is His encompassment (إحاطته) and knowledge of what he has done, His recording of it, and His recompense for it. He, the Exalted, said: **أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ** — *Is He who stands over every soul for what it has earned [like him who does not]? (13:33).*

It is also possible that "station" (المقام) is a noun of place (اسم مكان), and the annexation is possessive. What is meant is His station and position, the Exalted, in relation to His servant—namely, that He, the Exalted, is his Lord who manages his affairs. Part of managing his affairs is that He called him through the tongues of His messengers to faith and righteous deeds, and decreed that He would recompense him for what he did, whether good or evil. This is while He encompasses him, is with him, is All-Hearing of what he says, All-Seeing of what he does, and is All-Attentive (لطيف) and All-Aware (خبير).

Fear of Allah, the Exalted, might be fear of His punishment for disbelief in Him and disobedience to Him. The implication of this is that the worship of one who worships Him out of fear in this sense is intended to escape punishment, not purely for the sake of Allah. This is the "worship of slaves" (عبادة العبيد) who worship their masters out of fear of discipline. Similarly, the worship of one who worships Him out of greed for reward has as its goal the attainment of what the soul desires, rather than His Noble Face; this is the "worship of merchants" (عبادة التجار), as mentioned in the narrations, some of which have been cited previously.

The fear mentioned in the verse — **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ** — is apparently different from this fear. For this is fear of punishment, which is distinct from fear of His, the Exalted, standing over His servant regarding what he has done, or fear of His station in relation to His servant. It is a specific state of being affected (تأثر خاص) experienced by one who possesses nothing but smallness and insignificance in the presence of the court of Grandeur and Majesty (العظمة والكبرياء), and the manifestation of the effect of lowliness, humility, and annihilation (الاندكاك) before the absolute Might and Omnipotence (العزة والجبروت).

Worshipping Him, the Exalted, out of fear of Him in this sense is a form of submission (خضوع) to Him because He is Allah, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity (ذو الجلال والإكرام), not out of fear of His punishment nor greed for His reward. Rather, it involves sincerity (إخلاص) of action for His Noble Face. This meaning of fear is that with which Allah described His honored angels, who are infallible (معصومون) and secure from the punishment of opposition and the consequence of disobedience. He, the Exalted, said: **يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ** — *They fear their Lord [who is] above them (16:50).*

Thus, it becomes clear from what has preceded that those referred to by His saying: "And for him who fears (وَلِمَنْ خافَ)" are the people of sincerity (أهل الإخلاص) who are submissive to His Majesty, the Exalted, worshipping Him because He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), not out of fear of His punishment nor greed for His reward. It is not unlikely that they are the ones named "the foremost" (سابقين) in His saying: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً** — *and you will be three groups (56:7)* until He said: **وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — *And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones; they are the ones brought near [to Allah] (56:10-11).*

Regarding His saying: "two gardens" (جَنَّتانِ), it has been said: one of them is his residence and the place where his beloved ones visit him, and the other is the residence of his spouses and servants. It has also been said: two orchards, one orchard inside his palace and one orchard outside it. It has been said: two residences between which he moves to complete his pleasure. It has been said: a garden for his belief and a garden for his deeds. It has been said: a garden for performing acts of obedience and a garden for abandoning acts of disobedience. It has been said: a physical garden and a spiritual garden. These views — as you can see — have no evidence for any of them.

It has also been said: a garden as a reward and a garden as a favor (تفضل) bestowed upon him. This might be sensed from His saying, the Exalted: **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ** — *They will have therein whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more (50:35)*, as per what was mentioned in its commentary.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذَواتا أَفْنانٍ** — *abounding in branches (55:48).* *Dhawātā* (ذَواتا) is the dual of *dhāt* (ذات). *Afnān* (أَفْنانٍ) is either the plural of *fann* (فن) meaning "type" (النوع), so the meaning is: possessing types of fruits and the like; or it is the plural of *fanan* (فنن) meaning a fresh, supple branch (الغصن الرطب اللين), so the meaning is: possessing supple branches on their trees.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِما عَيْنانِ تَجْرِيانِ** — *In both of them are two flowing springs (55:50).* The two springs are left indefinite, which indicates the magnificence of their state.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِما مِنْ كُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ زَوْجانِ** — *In both of them are two kinds of every fruit (55:52)* meaning two types. It has been said: a type known to them which they saw in the world, and a type unknown which they did not see in the world. Others have said otherwise, but there is no indication in the speech for any of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى فُرُشٍ بَطائِنُها مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ** — *Reclining on couches lined with brocade (55:54)* etc. *Furush* (الفرش) is the plural of *firāsh* (فراش) — *couch or bedding*. *Batā’in* (البطائن) is the plural of *bitānah* (بطانة), which is the interior or inside of a thing, as opposed to *dhahā’ir* (الظهائر), the plural of *dhihārah* (ظهارة) — *the exterior*. *Istabraq* (الإستبرق) is thick silk. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "He mentioned the lining (البطانة) and did not mention the exterior (الظهارة) because the lining indicates that it has an exterior, and the lining is inferior to the exterior; thus it indicates that the exterior is superior to brocade." End quote.

His saying: **وَجَنَى الْجَنَّتَيْنِ دانٍ** — *and the fruit of both gardens is within reach (55:54).* *Janā* (الجنى) is the fruit that is gathered, and *dān* (دانٍ) is the active participle from *dunuww* (الدنو) meaning "closeness" (القرب); i.e., what is gathered from the fruits of the two gardens is near.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِنَّ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ** — *In them are maidens of restrained glances (55:56)* until the end of the verse. The pronoun in "in them" (فِيهِنَّ) refers to the couches (الفرش), though it has been allowed to refer to the gardens (الجنان), for they are gardens such that for every one of the friends of Allah there are two gardens. *Tarf* (الطرف) is the eyelid. What is meant by "restrained glances" is that they are content with their spouses and desire no one else.

His saying: **لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلا جَانٌ** — *whom no human has touched before them, nor any jinn (55:56).* *Tamt* (الطمث) is defloration and intercourse involving blood. The meaning is: no human or jinn has touched them through intercourse before their spouses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَأَنَّهُنَّ الْياقُوتُ وَالْمَرْجانُ** — *as if they were rubies and corals (55:58)* meaning in the purity of color, splendor, and radiance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ** — *Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness? (55:60).* This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for what has been mentioned of His, the Exalted, goodness toward them with the two gardens and the types of blessings and favors therein. It signifies that He, the Exalted, bestows this goodness upon them as a recompense for their goodness in fearing the station of their Lord.

The verse indicates that what they are given of the Garden and its bliss is a recompense for their deeds. As for what is understood from some verses that they are given a favor (فضلا) beyond the recompense for their deeds, these verses do not address that, unless it is said: goodness (الإحسان) is only complete when it exceeds what the benefactor was given. Thus, the absolute use of "goodness" in His saying: "except goodness (إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ)" implies an increase.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ** — *And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62).* The dual pronoun refers to the two gardens described in the previous verses. The meaning of "besides these two" (مِنْ دُونِهِما) is: lower in degree and less in merit and honor than them, even if they are similar to the two previous gardens in their blessings and favors. It has already been mentioned that the two previous gardens are for the people of sincerity who fear the station of their Lord; thus, these two gardens are for those below them among the believers who worship Allah, the Glorified, out of fear of the Fire or greed for the Garden, and they are the "Companions of the Right" (أصحاب اليمين).

It has also been said: the meaning of "besides these two" is "near to them." In that case, it is understood from the context that these two gardens are also for the people of the two gardens mentioned before. Some have even claimed that these two gardens are better than the previous two and that the attributes mentioned in them are more praiseworthy.

However, by reflecting on what we have presented regarding the meaning of "for him who fears the station of his Lord," and what is understood from His, the Exalted, speech that the people of the Garden are of two categories: the "brought near" (المقربون) who are the people of sincerity, and the "Companions of the Right," you will recognize the strength of the first view.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُدْهامَّتانِ** — *of a deep green (55:64).* *Idhihmām* (الادهيمام) comes from *duhmah* (الدهمة), which is the intensity of greenness such that it tends toward blackness, and it refers to the vibrancy of the tree.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِما عَيْنانِ نَضَّاخَتانِ** — *In both of them are two gushing springs (55:66)* meaning they are erupting, coming out of their source with force.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِما فاكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ** — *In both of them are fruits, date-palms and pomegranates (55:68).* What is meant by "fruits" and "pomegranates" is their trees, by the context of "date-palms."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ** — *In them are maidens, good and beautiful (55:70).* The pronoun in "in them" (فِيهِنَّ) refers to the gardens (الجنان) considering that they are two gardens from these two gardens. It has been said the pronoun refers to the four gardens mentioned in the verses, and it has been said the pronoun refers to the fruits, date-palms, and pomegranates.

"Good" (الخير) is most often used for internal qualities (المعاني), just as "beautiful" (الحسن) is most often used for external forms (الصور). Based on this, the meaning of "good and beautiful" is that they are good in their character and beautiful in their faces.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حُورٌ مَقْصُوراتٌ فِي الْخِيامِ** — *houris, secluded in pavilions (55:72).* *Khiyām* (الخيام) is the plural of *khaymah* (خيمة), which is a tent (الفسطاط). Their being "secluded in pavilions" means they are protected and not common, with no share in them for anyone other than their spouses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنْسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلا جَانٌ** — *whom no human has touched before them, nor any jinn (55:74).* Its meaning has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِيٍّ حِسانٍ** — *Reclining on green cushions and beautiful carpets (55:76).* In *al-Sihāh*: *Rafraf* (الرفرف) are green cloths from which sitting places are made. End quote. It has been said they are cushions (الوسائد), and other things have been said. *Khudr* (الخضر) is the plural of *akhdar* (أخضر) — *green* — an attribute of *rafraf*. *‘Abqarī* (العبقري) has been said to be carpets (الزرابي), or rugs (الطنافس), or embroidered cloths (الثياب الموشاة), or silk brocade (الديباج).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَبارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِي الْجَلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ** — *Blessed is the Name of your Lord, the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity (55:78).* This is a beautiful praise for Him, the Exalted, for the fact that both stages of existence — this world and the Hereafter — are filled with His blessings, favors, and benedictions descending from Him by His vast mercy. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by His blessed Name is "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) with which the Surah opened. *Tabārak* (التبارك) is the abundance of goodness and blessings that proceed [from Him].

Thus, His saying: "Blessed is the Name of your Lord" means: Blessed is Allah, who is named "the All-Beneficent," for He has poured forth these favors.

His saying: "the Possessor of Majesty and Generosity" (ذِي الْجلالِ وَالْإِكْرامِ) is an allusion to His being named by His Beautiful Names (أسماؤه الحسنى) and His being described by the descriptive meanings they indicate, and the attributes of Majesty (الجلال) — *the attributes of transcendence and awe* — and Beauty (الجمال) — *the attributes of grace and mercy*. The attributes of the Agent (الفاعل) have a manifestation in His acts and an effect therein by which the act is linked to its Agent. Thus, He, the Exalted, created the creation and organized the system because He is the Originator (بديع), the Creator (خالق), and the Beginner (مبدئ). He perfected the act because He is the All-Knowing (عليم) and All-Wise (حكيم). He recompensed the people of obedience with good because He is the All-Loving (ودود), the All-Appreciative (شكور), the All-Forgiving (غفور), and the Most Merciful (رحيم). And He recompensed the people of transgression with evil because He is the Avenger (منتقم) and Severe in Punishment (شديد العقاب).

Therefore, describing the Lord — whose vast mercy has been praised — as the "Possessor of Majesty and Generosity" is to indicate that His Beautiful Names and His Sublime Attributes have a role in the descent of blessings and goodness from Him, and that His blessings and favors bear the stamp of His Beautiful Names and His Sublime Attributes, Blessed and Exalted is He.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*: It has come in the report: "The creation will be surrounded by angels — and by a tongue of fire — then they will be called: **يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ** — *O company of jinn and humans! If you can...* until His saying: **يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُما شُواظٌ مِنْ نارٍ** — *There will be unleashed upon you a flame of fire...*"

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated from Mus‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Kulayb from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from Dawud al-Raqqi from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ** — *For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46).* He said: "**من علم أن الله يراه ويسمع ما يقول ـ ويعلم ما يعمله من خير أو شر ـ فيحجزه ذلك عن القبيح من الأعمال ـ فذلك الذي خاف مقام ربه ونهى النفس عن الهوى** — Whoever knows that Allah sees him and hears what he says — and knows what he does of good or evil — and that restrains him from ugly deeds — that is the one who fears the station of his Lord and forbids the soul from desire."

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Ibn Mani‘, al-Hakim in *Nawādir al-Usūl*, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Darda’: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ** — *For him who fears the standing before his Lord, there will be two gardens (55:46).* I said: "Even if he commits adultery and even if he steals, O Messenger of Allah?" The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said a second time: **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ**. I said: "Even if he commits adultery and even if he steals?" He said: "Yes, even if it be against the wishes of Abu al-Darda’."

**I say:** The narration is not without issues, for fear of His, the Exalted, station does not coexist with these destructive major sins. Indeed, it has been narrated from Abu al-Darda’ himself that which refutes this narration. In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Yasar, a client of the family of Mu‘awiyah, from Abu al-Darda’: regarding His saying: **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ**. He said: It was said: "O Abu al-Darda’, even if he commits adultery and even if he steals?" He replied: "**من خاف مقام ربه لم يزن ولم يسرق** — Whoever fears the station of his Lord does not commit adultery and does not steal."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ** — *maidens of restrained glances (55:56).* He said: "**الحور العين يقصر الطرف عنها من ضوء نورها** — The houris: the glance is restrained from them due to the brilliance of their light."

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father from his grandfather from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: **قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ**. He said: "**لا ينظرن إلا إلى أزواجهن** — They do not look except at their spouses."

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **كَأَنَّهُنَّ الْياقُوتُ وَالْمَرْجانُ** — *as if they were rubies and corals (55:58).* In the hadith: "**أن المرأة من أهل الجنة يرى مخ ساقها ـ من وراء سبعين حلة من حرير** — Indeed, the woman from the people of the Garden: the marrow of her shin is seen from behind seventy robes of silk."

**I say:** This meaning is reported in several narrations.

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyāshī*, with its chain of transmission from ‘Ali ibn Salim, who said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: "A verse in the Book of Allah is general (مسجلة)." I asked: "And what is it?" He said: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ** — *Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness? (55:60).* It applies to the disbeliever and the believer, the righteous and the wicked. Whoever has a favor done for him must reciprocate it, and reciprocation is not just to do as he did until he exceeds it — for if you do just as he did, the merit belongs to him for initiating."

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding His saying: **هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ**. A narration came from Anas ibn Malik who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse and said: "Do you know what your Lord says?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "Your Lord says: **هل جزاء من أنعمنا عليه بالتوحيد إلا الجنة؟** — Is the recompense of one whom We have favored with monotheism (التوحيد) anything but the Garden?"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding the verse, he said: "**ما جزاء من أنعمت عليه بالمعرفة إلا الجنة** — What is the recompense of one whom I have favored with gnosis (المعرفة) except the Garden?"

**I say:** The narration is reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). It is attributed in *al-Tawhīd* to Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his fathers from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — and its wording is: "**أن الله عز وجل قال : ما جزاء من أنعمت عليه بالتوحيد إلا الجنة** — Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'What is the recompense of one whom I have favored with monotheism except the Garden?'" It is attributed in *al-‘Ilal* to al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — and the wording is: "**هل جزاء من قال : لا إله إلا الله إلا الجنة؟** — Is the recompense of one who says 'There is no god but Allah' anything but the Garden?"

The narration is reported with its various wordings in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* through different paths from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). His saying: "whom I have favored" is an indication that the servant's goodness is, in reality, a goodness from Allah toward him.

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ** — *And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62).* From al-‘Ala’ ibn Siyabah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): I said to him: "People are surprised at us when we say: 'A group will come out of the Fire and enter the Garden' — they say to us: 'Will they then be with the friends of Allah in the Garden?'" He said: "O ‘Ali, indeed Allah says: **وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ** — *And besides these two there are two [other] gardens (55:62)*; they will not be with the friends of Allah."

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Musa from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): regarding His saying: **وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ** and His saying: **وَمِنْ دُونِهِما جَنَّتانِ**. He said: "**جنتان من ذهب للمقربين ـ وجنتان من ورق لأصحاب اليمين** — Two gardens of gold for the 'brought near' — and two gardens of silver for the 'Companions of the Right'."

**I say:** The two narrations support what we presented in the commentary of the two verses.

In it, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Ayyub who said: I asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about His saying: **مُدْهامَّتانِ** — *of a deep green (55:64).* He said: "**خضراوان** — Two very green ones."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of transmission to Yunus ibn Zabyan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: **نَضَّاخَتانِ** — *gushing (55:66).* He said: "**تفوران** — Erupting."

In it: regarding His saying: **فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ** — *In them are maidens, good and beautiful (55:70).* He said: "**جوار نابتات على شط الكوثر ـ كلما أخذت منها نبتت مكانها أخرى** — Maidens growing on the bank of al-Kawthar — whenever you take one of them, another grows in her place."

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding His saying: **خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ** — meaning women of good character and beautiful faces: it was narrated by Umm Salamah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Faqīh*, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "**الخيرات الحسان من نساء أهل الدنيا ـ وهن أجمل من الحور العين** — The 'good and beautiful' are from the women of the people of this world — and they are more beautiful than the houris."

In *Rawdat al-Kāfī*, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فِيهِنَّ خَيْراتٌ حِسانٌ**. He said: "**هن صوالح المؤمنات العارفات** — They are the righteous among the believing women who possess gnosis."

**I say:** There is some ambiguity in the application of the verse to the subject of these two narrations when considering its context.
